title: Protestant Reformation
description: Test your comprehension of the political and religious issues of the Reformation and Counter-Reformation.

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following does **NOT** describe John Calvin’s conception of Predestination?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Like Luther, Calvin believed in the inherent sinfulness of mankind and our inability to earn salvation through good works. Calvin believed election (salvation) was predetermined by God (and could not be gained through good works).

In effect this argues that the good works one does are the result of God’s grace and not any actions undertaken by an individual of their own free will. Similarly, if one is sinful, it is not the result of poor judgment but rather the outcome of God having withheld grace. In short, individuals do not control their destiny, that is God’s responsibility.

Calvin is discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4).

choices.content: faith is a gift from God, freely given and not something that can be earned
the choices one makes in life do not improve one’s chances of entering heaven
an individual’s actions accord with God’s will
the elect are chosen by God
election can be gained through good works

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following does **NOT** describe the Anglican Church?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
John Wyclif  had no direct involvement in the founding of the Anglican Church. He was a fourteenth-century reformer who translated parts of the Bible into English. 

The Anglican Church was founded in 16th century England, with the monarch, Henry VIII, as its leader (not the Pope). This occurred soon after Henry VIII was denied an annulment by the Pope.

These topics are discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4)

choices.content: it is the Church of England, founded in the 16th century
it was founded as a result of John Wyclif’s translation of the Bible into English
the King or Queen of England led the Anglican Church
it was founded soon after Henry VIII was denied an annulment by the Pope for his marriage to Catherine of Aragon
the Anglican Church does not answer to the Pope in Rome

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following is correct? The Edict of Nantes in 1598...

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Edict of Nantes promoted religious tolerance by granting the Huguenots (the Protestants in France) civil rights as a religious minority. Huguenots were French Protestants and were declared heretics and violently persecuted for much of the 16th century.

The Edict of Nantes is discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4)

choices.content: outlawed the translation of the Bible into the common languages
established the principle of “whose realm, his religion” in the Holy Roman Empire
declared John Calvin a heretic, forcing him to flee to Switzerland
established religious toleration in France between the Huguenots and the Catholics
made the practice of Protestantism illegal and unleashed the French Wars of Religion

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following was **NOT** a result of the Protestant Reformation?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
H. Luther and Zwingli met to discuss Holy Communion but were unable to reconcile their different interpretations.

However, it is true that some Protestants characterized the Pope as the Antichrist while some Roman Catholics characterized Luther as the Antichrist. The Reformation caused significant violence including against images (iconoclasm), the result of some Protestants’ concern that images were being directly worshipped as idols.

[This video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4) and [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation--the-counter-reformation--4-of-4) discuss these ideas.

choices.content: Protestant denominations including the Lutherans, Calvinists, Anglicans, and Anabaptists
violence between Protestants and Catholics
iconoclasm
attempts at reconciliation including the Peace of Augsburg and the Edict of Nantes
the reconciliation of Luther and Zwingli on the topic of Holy Communion

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following was **NOT** reaffirmed at the Council of Trent?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The Anabaptists believed in adult Baptism and were considered heretics by the Roman Catholic Church. 

The Council of Trent was an assembly of high officials in the Church who met (on and off for eighteen years) to deal with the issues raised by Luther and his followers. This assembly flatly rejected Luther’s new doctrines and and those of other Protestants such as the Anabaptists and strongly reaffirmed its doctrines. 

These topics are discussed in [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4) and [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation--the-counter-reformation--4-of-4)

choices.content: the effectiveness of indulgences for souls in purgatory
the importance of images in the church to teach the doctrines of the church and to deepen people’s faith
the ancient practice of adult baptism
the need for Church tradition and teaching in addition to the scriptures
that good works in addition to faith can help to secure a place in heaven

assessment_items.question_content:
Which statement about the 95 theses is false?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Luther’s 95 Theses were written in Latin and translated into German without Luther’s permission

In addition, the 95 Theses had been prompted in part by the sale of indulgences authorized by Pope Leo X to support the construction of Saint Peter’s basilica in Rome. Luther’s theses were written to begin a scholarly debate but, thanks in part to the invention of the printing press a half century earlier, his ideas spread rapidly.

Watch [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-Luther-2-of-4) to review the 95 Theses.

choices.content: the 95 Theses are largely concerned with the sale of indulgences
the 95 Theses were written in German
a thesis is a point of argument in a scholarly debate
the 95 Theses questioned the Pope’s motives
the 95 Theses were widely distributed thanks to the printing press

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following does **NOT** accurately describe Martin Luther?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Martin Luther was not English and his body was never exhumed and burned. 

He was a German monk and theologian who sparked the Protestant Reformation when he posted his 95 Theses on the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg in 1517. The 95 Theses were concerned primarily with the sale of indulgences and were informed by Luther’s sense of his own sinfulness and the sinfulness of mankind and our inability to ensure our own salvation through good works.

[These issues are covered in this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-Luther-2-of-4)

choices.content: he authored the 95 Theses which expressed grave concerns about the sale of indulgences
he was an English reformer whose body was exhumed and then burned for heresy
he sparked the Protestant Reformation
he experienced a spiritual crisis that consumed him with the fear that he could never overcome his sins
he was a monk and professor of theology in Wittenberg, Germany

assessment_items.question_content:
Which of the following beliefs were **NOT** part of Luther’s theology?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Luther believed that the body and blood of Christ were actually present in the bread and the wine of the Eucharist. 

In contrast with Luther, the Swiss reformer, Ulrich Zwingli taught that the bread and wine only symbolically represented the flesh and blood of Christ. However, Luther did not believe in transubstantiation (the transformation of the bread and wine *into* the body and blood of Christ). 

Luther did look to the Bible alone as the true source of Christ’s teaching. The Catholic Church, on the other hand, saw the teachings and traditions of the Church as another authoritative source. Luther also held that faith alone was the path to salvation, rejecting the Church’s idea that one could speed one’s way to heaven by undertaking good works (charitable activities). Luther also believed in what he called the priesthood of all believers, that is, that the priest and Pope had no special relationship with God.

Watch [this video](/humanities/history/1500-1600-Renaissance-Reformation/protestant-reformation/v/introduction-to-the-protestant-reformation-3-of-4) to review these ideas.

choices.content: that scripture was the sole source of authority for Christians
that faith alone was man’s only path to salvation
that the bread and the wine used for the sacrament of Communion only symbolically represented the flesh and the blood of Christ
that the priests and the Pope had no special relationship to God
